questions.prompt: Which sentence uses nominalisation CORRECTLY to increase formality?

options.text: They failed to implement the plan quickly.
The failure to implement the plan resulted in significant delays.
They didn't do the plan and it took forever.
The plan was not implemented in a quick way.

questions.explanation: Option (b) nominalises "failed" → "the failure" and uses impersonal construction (no named subject They). "Resulted in" is also a formal verb. (a) is verb-heavy and personal; (c) is informal; (d) avoids contractions but "in a quick way" is informal where "promptly" or "in a timely manner" would be formal.

questions.prompt: Nominalise the verb in brackets to complete the sentence formally: "The [decide] to restructure the department surprised many employees."

options.text: deciding
decided
decision
decide

questions.explanation: The nominalisation of "decide" is "decision" — a noun that can head the subject noun phrase: "The decision to restructure…". "Deciding" is a gerund (acceptable but less formal than the full nominalisation). "Decided" is the past tense and "decide" is the base form — neither can be a noun here.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a formal academic sentence that uses hedging language?

options.text: Evidence suggests that the intervention may have contributed to improved outcomes.
The data is definitely proof that the theory is right.
I think the results show the theory is probably right.
It's pretty clear the data backs up the theory.

questions.explanation: Option (a) uses hedging language (suggests, may have contributed) and impersonal construction (Evidence suggests…), with formal vocabulary (intervention, outcomes). (b) is overconfident and lacks hedging; (c) uses first person (I think); (d) uses informal vocabulary (pretty clear, backs up).

questions.prompt: Which feature makes this sentence LESS formal: "We think it's important to carry out more research."

options.text: The use of a complete sentence.
The word "research".
The use of the verb "think".
The use of first person ("We"), the contraction ("it's"), and the phrasal verb ("carry out").

questions.explanation: Three informal features combine: first-person We, the contraction it's (formal = it is), and the phrasal verb carry out (formal = conduct). "Research" is perfectly formal; having a complete sentence is not informal. Option (d) correctly identifies all three informal markers together.

questions.prompt: Find the MOST formal version of: "The manager got angry when he found out about the delay."

options.text: The manager got angry after finding out the delay.
The manager blew a fuse on discovering the delay.
The manager expressed considerable displeasure upon learning of the delay.
The manager was pretty mad when he found out about the delay.

questions.explanation: Option (c) nominalises the emotion (displeasure), uses formal vocabulary (considerable, upon learning of), and eliminates contractions and informal phrasal verbs. (a) still uses "got angry" and "finding out"; (b) uses the idiom "blew a fuse" which is informal; (d) adds the informal hedge "pretty mad".

questions.prompt:
Read: "The implementation of sustainable energy policies has been identified as a priority at the national level."
Which word in this sentence is an example of nominalisation?

options.text: sustainable
national
identified
implementation

questions.explanation: "Implementation" is the nominalisation of the verb "implement" — a noun formed from a verb by adding the suffix -ation. "Sustainable" is an adjective (derived from a verb but functioning adjectivally here); "identified" is a past participle verb; "national" is an adjective derived from a noun.
